hypothesis: Keltner bands define volatility‑adjusted trend bounds; a price break above the upper band with a strong ADX (>25) signals a reliable uptrend, while a break below the lower band with strong ADX signals a reliable downtrend. When ADX falls below 25 the market shifts to a weak‑trend regime, so we exit trend positions and avoid new entries until a new band breach occurs. This regime‑adaptation reduces whipsaws in ranging periods and captures strong moves in volatile conditions.

Keltner band breakouts identify strong trend regimes; when ADX is high (>25) the market is trending and a breakout above the upper band (or below the lower band) yields directional trades. When ADX falls below 20 the trend weakens and the price tends to revert toward the Keltner middle band, so a cross of the middle band in the appropriate direction gives a reversal trade. ATR‑based SL/TP scales risk to current volatility, limiting drawdown and protecting profits in both regimes.

A Keltner channel breakout combined with an ADX trend strength filter limits entries to genuine trend moves while ATR-based risk limits adapt to market volatility, reducing whipsaw losses that plagued the previous design.

A Keltner band breakout combined with an ADX trend filter and a minimum volatility requirement (ATR > 0.1% of price) eliminates whipsaw entries during low‑volatility periods. Exiting when the price retreats to the middle band or the trend weakens (ADX < 20) preserves capital while still capturing sustained moves, thereby reducing drawdown and improving Sharpe.
